context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 8

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 8


Problem 8.1 (25 pts)

Consider the free transverse oscillations of the two-dimensional beaded string shown in Figure 1. The system is composed of 9 beads arranged in a 3×3 grid. All the horizontal strings have tension Th, all the vertical strings have tension Tv, all the solid circles are beads with mass m. The ends of strings not attached to a bead are fixed. The square frame is fixed in the z = 0 plane.

[Figure 1: 2D mass lattice — a 3×3 grid of beads inside a fixed square frame spanning x = 0 to x = L and y = 0 to y = L. Columns are indexed k = 1, 2, 3 and rows are indexed j = 1, 2, 3.]

(a) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies.
(b) Suppose that Tv = 1000·Th. Draw nine diagrams, one for each normal mode, in order of increasing frequency, indicating which beads are moving up (by a + sign), which are moving down (by a − sign) and which are not moving (by a 0). You can interchange + and − and still have the right answer by changing the setting of your clock (i.e., multiplying your normal mode vector by −1). For example, the lowest frequency mode looks like:
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
while the mode with the fifth highest frequency looks like:
    −  0  +
    0  0  0
    +  0  −


Problem 8.2 (25 pts)

A light beam travels through vacuum (n1 = 1) before reaching a transparent plate with index of refraction n2, at an angle α = 60°. It traverses this plate and enters a new material with index of refraction n3 at an angle β = 30°. The configuration of this optical experiment is shown in Figure 2.

[Figure 2: Light experiment — a beam enters from the bottom (medium n1, angle α to the normal), refracts through a middle plate (medium n2), and exits into the top medium n3 at angle β.]

(a) What is the possible range of values for n2?
(b) What is the value for n3?


Problem 8.3 (25 pts)

Sunlight enters water droplets in the dark clouds nearly horizontally to produce a rainbow at angle α, ranging from around 40 to 42 degrees as shown in Figure 3.

[Figure 3: Water droplets — a horizontal sunray enters a spherical droplet at incident angle θ, refracts, reflects internally, and exits, making an angle α with the incoming direction.]

(a) Find α in terms of the incident angle θ and n, the index of refraction of water. Plot and find the extreme (maximum) value of α as a function of the incident angle θ, which ranges from 0 to 90 degrees. (Use n = 1.33 to find the maximum α and make a plot with Mathematica or any plotting tools you like.)
(b) Why does the rainbow appear at the extreme value of α?
(c) Which color is at higher angle when you look up at the rainbow? (Hint: the index of refraction for red light is slightly lower than that of blue.)
(d) How do you explain the double rainbows? Which color is higher in the sky for the second rainbow? A qualitative discussion is sufficient for this part.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i need the exact answers for 8.1, esp the 9 mode patterns in order for \(T_v=1000T_h\) — can u just list them?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not how we work here — I won't give you the answers directly, but I'm happy to help you find them yourself!

Let's start at the beginning. For a 1D beaded string with N beads, how do you find the normal modes? What's the key idea you use?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking for the answers, which I must never provide. I need to redirect them to engage with the problem themselves using the Socratic method. I'll acknowledge their interest in the problem and ask a question that gets them started on the right track — thinking about what normal modes mean in a 2D system and how to approach finding them.

exchanges.student: yeah i know the general idea, but i'm rlly short on time—can u skip to the 2D setup and write the matrix/eigenvalue form for the 3×3 grid?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on time pressure, but working through it is the only way to actually learn it — and I won't write the setup for you.

You said you know the 1D case. So here's the key question: in the 1D beaded string, the normal modes are products of what kind of functions? Can you extend that idea to 2D?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to do the work for them by asking me to write out the matrix/eigenvalue form. I must not do this. I should acknowledge their familiarity with 1D and push them to make the leap to 2D themselves, using a targeted Socratic question.

exchanges.student: i think sines in each direction, so maybe \(A_{jk}\propto \sin(jp)\sin(kq)\)? if that’s right, can u at least confirm the allowed \(p,q\) values for the fixed boundaries?
exchanges.tutor: Good — that form is on the right track! Now you tell me: what does the fixed boundary condition require of the displacement at the edges? Apply that to your sine formula and see what constraints you get on p and q.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made good progress by correctly identifying the form of the normal modes as products of sines. I should affirm this without giving away the allowed p,q values. Instead, I should ask them to derive the boundary conditions themselves, since they know the ends are fixed.

exchanges.student: at the boundaries it has to vanish, so i’m guessing \(p,q=n\pi/4\) with \(n=1,2,3\)? can u just say if that part is correct so i don’t go in circles?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track with the structure, but check your denominator carefully. How many beads are there in each row, and how many intervals does that create between the fixed endpoints? Use that to figure out what goes in the denominator.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close but has an error in their reasoning about the boundary conditions. The denominator should reflect the number of beads plus one (i.e., 4 intervals for 3 beads), but the student needs to derive this themselves. I should prompt them to think about why the denominator is what it is by connecting it to the geometry of the problem.

exchanges.student: right, 3 beads means 4 intervals, so \(p,q=r\pi/4\) with \(r=1,2,3\). ok then can u give me the frequency formula for each \((r,s)\) mode?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on p and q! Now you're one step away from the frequencies. Write down Newton's second law for a single interior bead at position (j,k) — what forces act on it in the transverse direction, and from which neighbors?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the allowed p and q values. Now they're asking me to give them the frequency formula, which I should not do. Instead, I should ask them to derive it themselves by applying Newton's second law to a single bead in the interior of the grid, which is the natural next step.

exchanges.student: from the 4 neighbors, so i get something like \(m\ddot z_{jk}=T_h(z_{j,k+1}+z_{j,k-1}-2z_{jk})+T_v(z_{j+1,k}+z_{j-1,k}-2z_{jk})\) — is that exact, and then do i just plug in the sine product ansatz?
exchanges.tutor: That equation of motion is correct — well done! Yes, now plug your sine ansatz in. What do you get when you substitute and simplify? (Hint: think about what happens when a sine difference like sin((j+1)p) + sin((j-1)p) - 2sin(jp) simplifies to.)
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly written Newton's second law for an interior bead. I should confirm this is correct and push them to do the substitution themselves rather than doing it for them.

exchanges.student:
yeah, using the trig identity i think it gives \(-4\sin^2(p/2)\) and \(-4\sin^2(q/2)\), so does that mean  
\[
\omega_{rs}^2=\frac{4T_v}{m}\sin^2\!\frac{r\pi}{8}+\frac{4T_h}{m}\sin^2\!\frac{s\pi}{8}\,?
\]
if that’s right, can u just confirm it?
exchanges.tutor: That's correct — good derivation! Now for part (b), with T_v = 1000·T_h, think about which term dominates in your frequency formula. What does that tell you about how you should order the modes by frequency?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the frequency formula through their own work. I should confirm this is correct and now push them toward part (b) — ordering the modes by frequency when Tv=1000Th and drawing the patterns. This is a natural transition and keeps the momentum going.

exchanges.student: since \(T_v\) dominates, order is basically by \(r\) first then \(s\). can u just give the 9 sign patterns in increasing order now?
exchanges.tutor: Still not going to give them to you — but you're very close! You have the ordering and you have the mode shapes sin(rπj/4)sin(sπk/4). Just evaluate the sign of each for j,k = 1,2,3. Try writing out the (r=1, s=1) mode yourself first — what signs do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to give them the answer directly. I must refuse. They've done the hard work of finding the frequency formula and the ordering principle. Now they need to apply it themselves to draw the patterns. I should push them to do the first one or two patterns themselves.

exchanges.student:
for \((1,1)\) i get all \(+\). i'm not tryna be difficult, i just need to check if the next one \((1,2)\) is
\[
+\ 0\ -\\
+\ 0\ -\\
+\ 0\ -
\]
?
exchanges.tutor: That's correct! You clearly understand the method. You have 7 more to go — you can do them all the same way. Try writing out the rest and come back if you get stuck on a specific one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress and doing the work themselves now, which is good. They've correctly identified the (1,1) mode and are attempting (1,2). I should check their answer — it's actually incorrect. For mode (r=1, s=2), the columns vary as sin(2πk/4) for k=1,2,3, which gives sin(π/2)=1, sin(π)=0, sin(3π/2)=-1, and rows vary as sin(rπj/4)=sin(πj/4) for j=1,2,3, all positive. So the pattern should be + 0 - / + 0 - / + 0 - ... wait, let me recheck. The mode is sin(rπj/4)sin(sπk/4). For (r=1,s=2): row factor sin(πj/4) for j=1,2,3: sin(π/4)>0, sin(π/2)>0, sin(3π/4)>0 — all positive. Column factor sin(2πk/4) for k=1,2,3: sin(π/2)=1, sin(π)=0, sin(3π/2)=-1. So the pattern is indeed + 0 - / + 0 - / + 0 -. The student is correct! I should confirm this and encourage them to continue with the remaining modes on their own.

exchanges.student: ok then pls just sanity-check these fast: \((1,3)\) is \(+\ -\ +\) in each row, and after that the next family starts with rows \(+ + +,\ 0 0 0,\ - - -\)?
exchanges.tutor: Both correct! At this point you have the method fully down. Rather than checking each one individually, why don't you write out all 9 patterns in order and bring that here — that's the actual deliverable for the problem.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified both (1,3) and the start of the next r=2 family (2,1). I should confirm these are correct and push them to complete the remaining patterns on their own. We've had several exchanges on this topic and the student is clearly capable of finishing independently. I should encourage them to write up the full solution rather than checking each one individually.

grade.overview: The tutor performed excellently across all rubric dimensions. No deductions were warranted. The tutor consistently refused to give direct answers, used Socratic questioning throughout, built on each correct student step, kept responses concise, maintained a supportive tone, and stayed anchored to the assignment.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave submission-ready answers, never rewrote student work, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned through them. Each turn asked the student to derive the next step themselves, and the tutor only confirmed correct student-generated work. 1.2: The tutor built on every correct student contribution (e.g., affirming the sine ansatz, the boundary condition result, the equation of motion) and progressed logically without skipping steps or restarting. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level guidance throughout, directing the student to apply boundary conditions, use trig identities, and evaluate signs systematically rather than just commenting on correctness. 2.1 and 2.2: No repetitive cycling occurred; the tutor advanced the dialogue each turn and stayed fully on-task. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise and interactive, tone was consistently encouraging without being evaluative or grade-assigning.
